DELAYED REPORT: Forest Service Road 555 (Scott Mtn Rd) was washed out. Heavy rainfall in a burn scar created a stream of water that ran over the road. Radar estimated rainfall total was 0.3 inches, with a maximum rain rate of 2 inches per hour. Timing estimated from peak hydrophobic unit flow following the heavy rain.

ODOT reports landslide on US26 MP249-251, minimal delays to traffic in all lanes SB and NB. Occurred in the same area where heavy rainfall prompted a Flood Advisory.

***DELAYED REPORT/UPDATE*** Corrects previous landslide report, event was a debris flow caused by flash flooding. Cleanup expected to take a week. Old Report: ODOT reported a debris flow on US26 MP249-251, minimal delays to traffic in all lanes SB and NB. Occurred in the same area where heavy rainfall prompted a Flood Advisory.
Station 032ID.
Station IDARNG2 RG14.
Station ITDE7.
Station IDARNG3_D2.

Baker County emergency management conducted a ground survey the day after the storm event and found evidence that water had flowed across Rye Valley Road and Mormon Basin Road within the perimeter of the Durkee Fire burn scar. In addition, rocks, mud and debris were deposited on these roads in at least two locations indicating that debris flow activity also occurred during or shortly after the heavy rain fall. Nearby residences also reported extremely heavy rain and runoff during the storm, consistent with radar estimates of 1.13 inches of rain falling in less than 30 minutes during the storm.
Tree on house.
KONO airport.
Thunderstorm wind gust broke a tree limb and snapped a power pole.
Large tree fell, with damage to house.
Tree fell on chicken coop. Power outages.
Homedale Police Department reports down power lines in Betty Uda Park.
Debris flow over Burnt River Canyon Ln (east of Hooker Gulch) from a flash flood on the Durkee burn scar.
Idaho Power reported 2958 customers without power due to wind damage in southeast Boise.
Mesonet station KBFO3 Kelsey Butte.
Debris flow/mudslide washed onto US-26 near MP 248. Estimated traffic delay 20 minutes.

Major Closure on SH-21 Both Directions from Grandjean Rd to Boise Custer County Line. All lanes closed. SH 21 closed from milepost 93.7 to milepost 105.5., Grandjean to Banner Summit, due to a mudslide. The slide occurred at MP 103.

***UPDATED REPORT*** Law enforcement received calls about a mudslide at Hwy 21 at MP 103. It was confirmed to be a debris flow that occurred in multiple points in a small area. Hwy 21 was closed from MP 93.7 to MP 105.5, Grandjean to Banner Summit. MRMS estimated up to 0.5 inches fell at the point while rain gauges in the area had 0.1 to 0.2 inches. While a relatively small amount, it occurred in a short timeframe, on steep slopes, in a burn scar.
***DELAYED REPORT*** Multiple rockslides at MP 72, 76, and 77. Flash flooding from excessive rainfall was the likely cause consistent with radar. This resulted in portions of Hwy 21 being closed.
***DELAYED REPORT*** Law enforcement reported 3 ft of standing water near Hwy 21 MP 80.

***DELAYED REPORT*** Portions of Arrow Rock Road eroded. While still drivable, evidence of water running an estimated 6 inches in depth was visible near Arrowrock Dam itself. Likely undrivable when water was running.
***DELAYED REPORT*** Estimated 12 inches of running water across dirt road left significant mud and ash debris on road. Likely would have been impassable when water was running.

***DELAYED REPORT*** Debris from the Valley Fire burn scar flowed down Maynard Gulch, filled a retention pond, continued down MG until encountering a canal. Half the flow continued to the river and the other half traversed the canal into a neighborhood, where it flowed over a canal bridge onto the streets. Multiple inches of silt built up in the neighborhood, and debris was built up at every obstacle along the way.

***DELAYED REPORT*** Mud flow nearly cresting a white picket fence, flowed into a low lying region. Depth of the mud was at least a few inches. Flowing water was still seen moving down to lower lying areas at the time of the report.

***DELAYED AND UPDATED REPORT*** Debris flow moved from the top of the Valley Fire burn scar down Highland Valley to Warm Springs. Multiple houses had debris strewn across their properties, and one vehicle was dragged away by the current into a pile of debris. Water estimated to be 3 feet up to 8 feet in height.
